description: Evaluation progress tracker — 0-100 undervalued score for 74 semiconductor supply chain companies

companies.name: Tokyo Seimitsu Co., Ltd.

B.note: Scored from company descriptions and graph.json

F.note: Scored from isForexSensitive, client arrays, descriptions

companies.investmentThesis: Tokyo Seimitsu (Accretech) occupies dual leadership positions: semiconductor probers (testing wafer-level ICs) and precision coordinate measuring machines (CMM). Its probers compete directly with Teradyne and form an oligopoly in advanced node testing. At PER 21.3x vs the G1 equipment peer median of 26.3x, the stock trades at a meaningful discount despite 11.8% revenue growth and 50% foreign ownership — indicating institutional recognition of quality. The stock sits near its 52-week high (JPY 15,820 vs high JPY 16,170), signaling momentum. China revenue at 34% is an elevated geopolitical risk. No active buyback reduces near-term capital return appeal. Catalyst: continued AI-driven capex at TSMC and Samsung driving prober demand. [Confidence: High — scored on 81.5% data coverage]

companies.name: Tokyo Electron Limited

B.note: B4 updated from graph.json; others from prior scoring

F.note: Scored from isForexSensitive, client arrays, descriptions

companies.investmentThesis: Tokyo Electron (TEL) is Japan's largest semiconductor equipment company and the global #3 overall (after ASML and Applied Materials), with leading positions in coater-developers, thermal processing, and etch systems. Its critical-node score (B4=5/5) reflects the highest supply chain centrality among equipment makers in this database. Financially, TEL delivers exceptional results: JPY 17.4T market cap, 32.8% revenue growth, 28.7% OPM, with an active buyback program and 1.27% dividend yield. At PER 29.1x — only a modest premium to peers given the growth rate — the stock is reasonably priced for a secular compounder. Foreign ownership at 59.5% reflects global institutional conviction. China revenue at ~30% is the key geopolitical risk. The world's AI infrastructure buildout is TEL's multi-year structural tailwind. [Confidence: High — 86.2% data coverage, complete scoring]

companies.name: Lasertec Corporation

B.note: Scored from company descriptions and graph.json

F.note: Scored from isForexSensitive, client arrays, descriptions

companies.investmentThesis: Lasertec is the sole global supplier of EUV photomask inspection systems — there is no substitute. Every EUV-enabled chip (Apple, NVIDIA, AMD, Qualcomm at 7nm and below) requires masks inspected on Lasertec equipment before production. This creates an absolute monopoly with perfect moat scores (B=25/25) and a critical-node position. The financial profile validates the moat: OPM 42.88% (best-in-class for equipment), revenue growth 32.5% YoY, with the stock pulling back 23% from its 52-week high — creating an attractive re-entry point. PER 33.1x appears elevated but is modest for a secular monopolist growing at 30%+. The primary risks are customer concentration (TSMC and Samsung dominate demand) and USD/JPY forex exposure. [Confidence: High — 81.5% data coverage, complete scoring]

B.note: Scored from company descriptions and graph.json

F.note: Scored from isForexSensitive, client arrays, descriptions

companies.investmentThesis: Fuso Chemical holds a near-monopoly in high-purity colloidal silica abrasives used in chemical mechanical planarization (CMP), a mandatory step in semiconductor manufacturing. Its PER of 17.1x sits at a discount to the G2 materials peer median, while PBR of 2.01x is modest for a niche monopolist. OPM of 17.8% and 7% revenue growth are solid for a specialty materials producer. As the most reliably scored company in the top 10 (81.5% data coverage, High confidence), this is the highest-conviction pick: a structurally protected business at a reasonable valuation with no forex sensitivity, capacity expansion plans, and strong policy tailwinds from Japan's semiconductor industrial policy. Key risk: small market cap (JPY 220B) limits institutional liquidity. [Confidence: High]

B.note: Scored from company descriptions and graph.json

F.note: Scored from isForexSensitive, client arrays, descriptions

companies.investmentThesis: Shin-Etsu Chemical is the world's largest producer of silicon wafers (>30% global share) and a dominant force in PVC and specialty silicones. Its semiconductor wafer business is deeply embedded in the global fab supply chain — every major logic and memory chipmaker relies on Shin-Etsu substrates, creating near-irreplaceable switching costs. The company holds a critical-node position (B4=5/5) reflecting its supply chain centrality. With 13 analyst buy ratings and capacity expansion underway to serve AI-driven wafer demand, the structural tailwinds are strong. The primary risk is USD/JPY sensitivity and lack of publicly available valuation data limiting score precision. Conviction is moat-driven pending financial data confirmation. [Confidence: Low — financial data incomplete]

companies.name: NVIDIA Corporation

B.note: B4 updated from graph.json; others from prior scoring

F.note: Scored from isForexSensitive, client arrays, descriptions

companies.investmentThesis: NVIDIA is the essential AI infrastructure company: its H100/H200/Blackwell GPUs and the CUDA software ecosystem form an entrenched monopoly in AI compute that no competitor has successfully displaced despite massive investment from AMD, Intel, and hyperscaler custom silicon programs. In the context of this supply chain map, NVIDIA is the demand anchor — its GPU production drives demand for every company in this database: TEL for CVD/etch tools, Lasertec for mask inspection, Shin-Etsu for wafers, Stella Chemifa for HF etchants, Daifuku for fab logistics. Perfect moat score (B=25/25). The core investment thesis is that NVIDIA's dominance sustains elevated semiconductor equipment and materials demand for 3-5 years, making it the demand-side anchor of the entire supply chain thesis. As a USD-listed mega-cap ($4.65T), it represents a different risk/return profile from the Japanese names in this database. [Confidence: Very Low in JPY scoring context — financial data in USD, not JPY-native]

companies.investmentThesis: Maruzen Petrochemical is a specialty chemical producer supplying base materials for semiconductor photoresists and other process chemicals. It achieves maximum scores on market share (10/10) and switching costs (5/5) within its niche — photoresist base chemicals are purity-critical and qualification cycles at fabs take years, making substitution prohibitively costly. As semiconductor lithography advances to EUV and beyond, demand for ultra-high-purity photoresist materials compounds. Policy tailwinds from Japan's semiconductor industrial policy (Rapidus, TSMC Kumamoto) provide domestic demand uplift. The thesis rests on structural moat; financial validation is the key next step to confirm valuation. [Confidence: Very Low — no financial data available]

companies.name: PILLAR Corporation

A.note: PER 21.54 vs G1 median 31.59 (0.68x); PBR 2.42 vs 2.49 (0.97x); EY spread 3.1%. Stock rallied +119% in 1yr, reducing valuation discount. Scored 3/4; pro-rata rescaled to 30

B.note: 90%+ world share in semi cleaning fittings (monopoly). S-300 fitting is de facto standard — Entegris licenses it. 14 client edges in graph (SCREEN, TEL, Ebara, Shibaura, Kokusai, CKD, Samco, TSMC, Samsung, Intel, SK Hynix, SMIC, Hua Hong, SEMES)

C.note: Rev growth -1.05% (FY2025 trough, Q3 FY2026 +8.14% YoY recovery). OPM 19.85% vs G1 14.9% (1.33x). ROE 11.78% (below 12% threshold). AI/HPC driving cleaning equipment demand

D.note: Foreign ownership 21.28% vs expected 45% for B>=18 company (gap 23.7pp). Active buyback (51.2k shares completed, 1M authorized). Trading at 99% of 52W high

E.note: 2 Buy / 0 Hold / 0 Sell, target 8,215 JPY (1.3% upside — analysts lagging rally). Malaysia subsidiary Oct 2025. Japan semi policy support

F.note: 30% direct overseas (>60% incl indirect). SCREEN 16.6% of rev. China ~10% via subsidiaries

companies.notes: Updated 2026-02-22 with comprehensive drill-down: 14 client relationships mapped (was 2), all metric gaps filled. Stock rallied +119% in 1yr reducing valuation discount from A=26.2 to A=20.0, offset by higher B4 centrality (1→4) and D1 foreign ownership gap (null→5).
companies.investmentThesis:
THE HIDDEN MONOPOLY: PILLAR Corporation holds arguably the most under-appreciated monopoly in the global semiconductor supply chain: 90%+ world market share in fluororesin fittings for semiconductor wet cleaning equipment. The Super 300 (S-300) fitting, PILLAR's flagship product, has become the de facto industry standard — so dominant that Entegris (ENTG, $17B market cap), the world's leading semiconductor fluid handling company, licenses PILLAR's S-300 technology rather than developing a competing standard. When a competitor of that scale chooses to license rather than compete, it is the ultimate validation of a monopoly position. Every wet cleaning, etching, and CMP tool shipped by SCREEN, Tokyo Electron, Lam Research, Applied Materials, or Ebara globally requires PILLAR's fluororesin fittings, valves, pumps, and tubing. The company earns a near-perfect moat score (B=24/25).

WHY FLUORORESIN FITTINGS MATTER: Semiconductor wet processes use ultra-aggressive chemicals — hydrofluoric acid, sulfuric acid, hydrogen peroxide, ammonia — at extreme concentrations and temperatures. Fluororesin (PFA/PTFE) fittings are the only materials that can withstand these environments without contaminating the wafer. At sub-7nm geometries, even parts-per-trillion contamination from fittings or tubing can destroy chip yields. PILLAR has spent 100 years (founded 1924, originally as Nippon Pillar Packing) perfecting the purity and dimensional precision of fluororesin components. The S-300 fitting system incorporates PILLAR's proprietary molding, welding, and surface finishing technologies that achieve semiconductor-grade cleanliness. Requalification of alternative fittings at a live fab would require 12-18 months and carry unacceptable contamination risk — switching costs are effectively infinite for existing tool platforms.

CLIENT ECOSYSTEM: PILLAR serves the semiconductor ecosystem through two channels. First, as an OEM supplier to equipment makers: SCREEN Holdings (CMP-0003, largest customer at 16.6% of revenue / 9.65B JPY), Tokyo Electron (CMP-0004), Ebara Corporation (CMP-0036), Shibaura Mechatronics (CMP-0059), Kokusai Electric (CMP-0011), CKD (CMP-0043), and Samco (CMP-0060) all embed PILLAR components into their tools. Second, through a global subsidiary network that supplies maintenance and replacement parts directly to fabs: PILLAR Taiwan (Kaohsiung factory, serving TSMC), PILLAR Korea (Seoul, serving Samsung/SK Hynix/SEMES), PILLAR Shanghai + Pillar Technology Chuzhou (serving SMIC/Hua Hong), and PILLAR America (Fremont CA, serving Intel). Additionally, through the Entegris licensing relationship, PILLAR fittings are embedded in Applied Materials, Lam Research, and KLA tools — meaning PILLAR's total addressable reach extends far beyond its direct client list. The company has 14 mapped client relationships in the supply chain graph.

SEGMENT STRUCTURE: The Electronic Equipment segment (67% of consolidated sales) is the semiconductor pure-play — PILAFLON fluororesin fittings, pumps, valves, and tubing for wet processes. This segment drives the investment thesis. The Industrial Equipment segment (33%) produces mechanical seals, gaskets, and packing for petrochemical, power generation, and automotive applications. This segment provides earnings stability and cyclical diversification. In April 2023, PILLAR acquired Tanken Seal Seiko for 6.6B JPY, adding carbon-based mechanical seal capabilities and strengthening the industrial business. The dual-segment structure is an often-overlooked advantage: in semiconductor downturns, the industrial segment floors the earnings decline.

FINANCIAL PROFILE: PILLAR's profitability metrics stand out among G1 Equipment peers. Operating margin at 19.85% is 1.33x the G1 peer median of 14.9%, reflecting the pricing power of a monopoly supplier. ROE at 11.78% is solid though slightly below the 12% excellence threshold (depressed by conservative balance sheet management). The balance sheet carries a net cash position of 9.7B JPY (424 JPY/share, ~5% of market cap), providing downside protection. 10-year revenue CAGR of 10.34% and net profit CAGR of 15.37% demonstrate consistent compounding — the profit CAGR exceeding the revenue CAGR by 5pp reflects operating leverage from the monopoly position. FY2025/3 revenue was 58.0B JPY (-1.05% YoY), marking a cyclical trough. Q3 FY2026/3 showed +8.14% YoY recovery. FY2026 guidance has been revised upward: operating income +16.5%, dividend raised to 130 JPY (yield 1.60%).

VALUATION — STILL CHEAP AFTER THE RALLY: The stock has rallied +119% over the past year (from 2,810 to 8,110 JPY), one of the strongest moves in the Japanese semiconductor supply chain. Despite this, PILLAR remains attractively valued relative to G1 Equipment peers: PER at 21.54x is a 32% discount to the G1 median of 31.59x — the deepest PER discount among monopoly-quality companies in our coverage. PBR at 2.42x is essentially at parity with the G1 median of 2.49x. Forward PER at 19.24x is even more compelling. The valuation discount persists because PILLAR was historically classified as an 'industrial seals' company, and many equity databases still categorize it under machinery or industrial components rather than semiconductor equipment. This misclassification suppresses institutional discovery. The earnings yield spread of 3.1% over JGBs provides a reasonable margin of safety.

FOREIGN OWNERSHIP GAP — THE PRIMARY CATALYST: At 21.28%, foreign institutional ownership is dramatically below the expected ~45% for a company with near-monopoly moat scores (B=24/25). This 23.7 percentage-point gap is the single strongest undervaluation signal in our framework. For comparison, SCREEN Holdings has ~40% foreign ownership, Tokyo Electron has ~55%, and Advantest has ~60%. PILLAR's gap exists because: (1) it was a 'Nippon Pillar Packing' until its June 2024 rebrand — not exactly a name that screams semiconductor monopoly to foreign fund managers; (2) market cap at 185B JPY (~$1.2B) sits below the radar of many large-cap semiconductor funds; (3) equity databases frequently miscategorize it. As awareness grows, the structural bid from foreign institutions should provide sustained buying pressure. Free float at 74.9% is ample to absorb this demand.

CAPACITY EXPANSION & GEOGRAPHIC REACH: PILLAR established a new Malaysia subsidiary in October 2025, its first manufacturing footprint in Southeast Asia, positioning for the growing semiconductor ecosystem in Malaysia (Intel Penang, Infineon Kulim, multiple OSAT facilities). Global subsidiaries now span Taiwan, Korea, China, USA, and Malaysia. This expansion directly tracks the geographic diversification of semiconductor manufacturing away from East Asian concentration.

CATALYSTS: (1) Foreign ownership gap — 23.7pp of structural institutional buying potential, the strongest near-term catalyst. (2) Revenue recovery — Q3 FY2026 shows +8.14% YoY growth after the FY2025 trough, with full-year guidance revised upward. (3) AI/HPC capex boom — every AI accelerator (NVIDIA H100/B200, AMD MI300, custom ASICs) requires advanced fab capacity, which requires cleaning equipment, which requires PILLAR fittings. (4) Global fab buildout — TSMC Arizona/Kumamoto/Dresden, Intel Ohio/Germany, Samsung Taylor TX, Rapidus Chitose all need new cleaning tools equipped with PILLAR components. (5) Malaysia subsidiary (Oct 2025) unlocks Southeast Asian revenue. (6) Active buyback program — 1M shares authorized, 51.2k completed, signaling management's view that shares are undervalued. (7) Potential index inclusion or reclassification as 'semiconductor equipment' could trigger forced buying.

RISKS: (1) SCREEN concentration — at 16.6% of revenue and growing (was 9% in FY2021), PILLAR is increasingly dependent on SCREEN's wet cleaning equipment sales trajectory. A SCREEN downturn would directly impact PILLAR. (2) Entegris licensing — while the licensing relationship validates the S-300 standard, it also means Entegris accumulates manufacturing know-how; a future termination of the license agreement could create a well-capitalized competitor, though S-300's installed base and switching costs make this a low-probability risk. (3) Chinese competition — PILLAR Shanghai and Chuzhou operations (~10% of revenue from China) face the risk of local Chinese fluororesin fitting makers developing competitive products as China's semiconductor self-sufficiency drive intensifies. ACM Research (Chinese/US cleaning equipment maker) is a growing customer but could eventually backward-integrate. (4) Forex sensitivity — with >60% of revenue tied to overseas semiconductor production (30% direct exports, remainder indirect via equipment makers), yen appreciation would compress margins. (5) Cyclicality — semiconductor equipment spending is inherently cyclical; the current AI capex boom will eventually normalize. (6) Raw material dependency — primary fluororesin supplier Daikin Industries could face supply disruptions or price increases.

CONCLUSION: PILLAR is the highest-conviction name in our coverage universe. The combination of (a) a genuine monopoly position (90%+ share, S-300 de facto standard, Entegris license validation), (b) the largest foreign ownership gap among monopoly-quality companies (23.7pp), and (c) still-attractive valuation despite the rally (PER 21.5x at a 32% discount to peers) creates a rare convergence of moat, catalyst, and price. The 10-year net profit CAGR of 15.37%, 19.85% operating margin, and net cash balance sheet confirm operational excellence. The June 2024 corporate rebrand from 'Nippon Pillar Packing' to 'PILLAR Corporation' was a deliberate signal that management intends to reposition the company's identity toward its semiconductor business — this should accelerate institutional discovery. At the current score of 72.0/100, this is the top-ranked G1 Equipment company in our framework. Suitable for a high-conviction position with current entry or on pullbacks to 7,000 JPY (18x forward PER). The rally has reduced but not eliminated the opportunity; the foreign ownership catalyst alone could sustain further appreciation over 12-18 months. [Score: 72.0/100 | Confidence: High — 100% data coverage]

companies.name: Stella Chemifa Corporation

A.note: PER 21.62 vs G2 median 17.0 (1.27x premium); PBR 1.39 vs 1.32 (1.05x); EY spread 3.1%. Stock rallied +42% in past year. Scored 3/4; pro-rata rescaled to 30

B.note: Perfect score. 12-nine purity HF — only Stella and Morita can achieve this globally. Irreplaceable duopoly. 10 fab clients in graph. Japan 2019 export controls proved strategic leverage

C.note: FY2025/3 revenue +19.2% (corrected from -0.9% which was FY2026 guidance). OPM 12.0% (actual, was incorrectly 18% EBITDA) vs G2 15.19%. ROE 6.5% (recovered from 0.3% one-time impairment in FY2024/3). Target ROE 8%+ by FY2028/3

D.note: Foreign ownership 25.93% vs expected 45% for B>=18 (gap 19.1pp). Active buyback (100%+ TSR target in 4th MTMP). Free float ~57%. Trading at 89% of 52W high

E.note: 1 analyst, Strong Buy but target 4,800 vs price 5,290 (stock exceeded target). Q3 OP progress 88.9% suggests upward guidance revision. Expanding capacity via China JVs

F.note: 58% overseas sales. End-user fabs diversified (10 fabs). But 2 distributors (Maruzen Chemicals, Mitsubishi Chemical) are named top customers. China supply chain risk (fluorite sourcing, JV operations)

companies.notes: Updated 2026-02-22 with comprehensive drill-down: 10 fab clients mapped (was 5), all metric gaps filled. Key corrections: ROE was 0.3% due to one-time 2.3B JPY impairment (FY2024/3), now recovered to 6.5%. OPM corrected from 18% (EBITDA) to actual 12.0%. Revenue growth corrected from -0.9% (FY2026 guidance) to +19.2% (FY2025/3 actual). Stock rallied +42%, reducing valuation discount. PBR revealed at 1.39 (vs G2 median 1.32 — essentially at parity).
companies.investmentThesis:
STRUCTURAL MOAT: Stella Chemifa operates in one of the semiconductor industry's tightest duopolies. Globally, only Stella and privately-held Morita Chemical Industries can produce twelve-nine purity (99.9999999999%) hydrofluoric acid — the grade required for etching sub-7nm logic, 3D NAND vertical channels, and EUV mask cleaning. Together they supply 90%+ of Japanese domestic HF demand and dominate the highest-purity tier globally. The global electronic-grade HF market ($1.39B in 2025, projected $1.87B by 2030 at 6.0% CAGR) has fewer than 15 capable producers, with the top 5 controlling ~70% of supply. Stella achieves a perfect moat score (B=25/25): monopoly-grade market share, extreme switching costs (requalification of ultra-pure chemicals takes 12-18 months per fab), and a century of fluorine chemistry know-how that cannot be replicated quickly.

GEOPOLITICAL LEVERAGE: Japan's July 2019 export restrictions on HF, photoresist, and fluorinated polyimide to South Korea demonstrated the strategic weaponization potential of this supply chain. Samsung and SK Hynix were unable to find alternative 12-nine HF sources and were forced into rapid diversification programs that took years. This episode permanently elevated Stella's strategic visibility and underscored the irreplaceability of Japanese ultra-high-purity chemicals.

CLIENT BASE & REVENUE STRUCTURE: Stella supplies virtually every major semiconductor fab globally: TSMC, Samsung, Intel, SK Hynix, Micron, Kioxia, SMIC, Hua Hong, UMC, and GlobalFoundries (10 fabs mapped in the supply chain graph). Semiconductor chemicals account for 57.8% of revenue, with the balance split between energy/battery materials (~15%, growing fastest), optical/general chemicals, and a transportation subsidiary. Two named major customers — Maruzen Chemicals (130-year-old Osaka chemical distributor) and Mitsubishi Chemical (buys LiPF6 for Li-ion battery electrolyte) — are disclosed in securities filings. Overseas sales represent 58% of revenue, providing geographic diversification across Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, and the USA.

BATTERY OPTIONALITY: Beyond semiconductors, Stella manufactures LiPF6 (lithium hexafluorophosphate), the critical electrolyte salt in Li-ion batteries. The supply chain runs: Stella (LiPF6) → Mitsubishi Chemical (Sol-Rite electrolyte formulation) → Panasonic (battery cells) → Tesla/EV OEMs. This creates optionality on the EV mega-trend without requiring separate capex. The energy segment (~15% of revenue) roughly doubled in FY2025/3 and is the fastest-growing division. Quzhou BDX, Stella's Chinese subsidiary in Zhejiang, manufactures LiPF6 locally. Chinese competitor Do-Fluoride (002407.SZ) also produces LiPF6, making this a directly contested market.

ADDITIONAL BUSINESSES: Stella is Japan's sole manufacturer of BF3 (boron trifluoride) gas for organic synthesis. Its enriched boron-10 technology serves nuclear fuel storage and, via subsidiary Stella Pharma, enables BNCT (Boron Neutron Capture Therapy) cancer treatment — a novel oncology modality. These are small but demonstrate deep fluorine chemistry capabilities.

FINANCIAL PROFILE: FY2025/3 showed strong recovery: revenue 36.29B JPY (+19.2% YoY), operating profit 4.34B JPY (+59.4%). ROE recovered to 6.5% from 0.3% (depressed by a one-time 2.3B JPY impairment charge in FY2024/3). The 4th Medium-Term Management Plan targets ROE of 8%+ by FY2028/3 and a total shareholder return ratio of 100%+ over three years (dividends + buybacks). Dividend yield stands at 3.21% — generous for a materials company. Balance sheet is clean with PBR of 1.39x (essentially at G2 peer median of 1.32x). Q3 FY2026/3 operating profit progress rate of 88.9% strongly suggests conservative full-year guidance is ripe for upward revision, as analyst consensus EPS (260.85 JPY) significantly exceeds company guidance (228.5 JPY).

VALUATION CONCERN: The stock has rallied +42% over the past year (from 3,130 to 5,290), reducing the valuation case materially. PER at 21.62x now sits at a 27% premium to the G2 Materials peer median (17.0x), reflecting the market's belated recognition of the moat. PBR at 1.39x is roughly at parity with peers. Earnings yield spread of 3.1% over JGBs is modest. The stock currently trades above the sole analyst's target of 4,800 JPY.

CATALYSTS: (1) Foreign ownership gap — at 25.9% vs an expected 45%+ for a monopoly-grade business with perfect moat scores, there is ~19pp of potential institutional buying as global funds discover this name. (2) Guidance revision — Q3 progress rate virtually guarantees an upward revision of FY2026 guidance, likely accompanied by a dividend increase. (3) Global fab buildout — TSMC Arizona/Kumamoto/Dresden, Intel Ohio/Germany, Samsung Taylor TX, Rapidus Chitose, and Micron Hiroshima expansion all drive incremental HF demand. (4) EV battery electrolyte growth via LiPF6.

RISKS: (1) Chinese EG-HF producers, particularly Do-Fluoride New Materials (002407.SZ), have reached UP-SSS (G5) grade — the highest purity level — threatening the Stella/Morita duopoly in the medium term. (2) Raw material dependency: fluorite (CaF2) sourcing is 60%+ from China; while Stella has diversified to Europe and Mexico, Chinese export restrictions on fluorite could disrupt supply. (3) ROE of 6.5% remains below the company's own cost of equity (7-8%), indicating the business is not yet earning its hurdle rate. (4) Only 1 sell-side analyst covers the stock, creating an information gap and potentially thin liquidity during corrections. (5) Yen appreciation would compress overseas earnings (58% of sales are international).

CONCLUSION: Stella Chemifa is a 'moat first, valuation second' story. The B=25/25 score reflects genuine irreplaceability — this is one of only two companies on Earth that can make the highest-purity HF. However, the market has partially caught up: at 21.6x PER, this is no longer cheap on a trailing basis. The investment case now rests on (a) the foreign ownership gap as a structural catalyst, (b) upcoming guidance revisions, (c) the secular tailwind from global fab expansion, and (d) LiPF6 battery optionality. Position sizing should reflect the elevated valuation and the emerging Chinese competitive threat. Suitable for a medium-conviction position with entry on dips below 4,800 JPY (analyst target) or on confirmation of FY2026 guidance revision. [Score: 60.5/100 | Confidence: High — 100% data coverage]

companies.name: Organo Corporation

A.note: PER 27.1 vs G1 median 31.59 (0.86x, modest discount); PBR 5.85 vs 2.49 (2.35x, extreme premium); EY spread 2.2%. Stock rallied +134% in 1yr. Scored 3/4; pro-rata rescaled to 30

B.note: 70-80% of TSMC water treatment plants, 100% for <=10nm nodes. ~100 UPW plants across 8 countries. #2 global semi UPW (20-30% share). TSMC 2024 Excellent Performance Award. Critical bottleneck — UPW quality directly determines chip yield

C.note: Revenue +8.6%. OPM 19.1% vs G1 14.9% (1.28x). ROE 21.7% (nearly 3x industry avg 7.6%). FY2026 guidance: orders 185B (+22.3%), revenue 175B (+7.2%), OP 36B (+15.7%)

D.note: Foreign ownership 22.5% vs expected 45% for B>=18 (gap 22.5pp). Tosoh holds 44% → free float only 54%. No confirmed buyback. Near 52W high (98%)

E.note: 2 Buy, target 16,250 vs price 16,620 (stock exceeded target). Massive capacity expansion: TSMC Kumamoto/Arizona/Dresden, Rapidus 2nm fab (Organo will own/operate UPW plant). Japan semi policy support

F.note: TSMC = 14% of revenue but 70-80% of semiconductor UPW business. Deep Taiwan/China exposure (29.7% Greater China). Geopolitical risk: TSMC concentration + China revenue

companies.notes: Updated 2026-02-22 with comprehensive drill-down: clients expanded (SMIC, Hua Hong added as probable Chinese clients), all metric gaps filled. PBR revealed at 5.85x — extremely expensive vs G1 median 2.49x. Stock rallied +134% in 1yr. Key discovery: TSMC is 14% of revenue but Organo holds 100% of TSMC sub-10nm UPW plants. Rapidus confirmed as new client. Samsung goes to Nomura Micro Science, not Organo.
companies.investmentThesis:
STRUCTURAL MOAT — THE TSMC LOCK-IN: Organo Corporation holds one of the most extraordinary customer lock-ins in the semiconductor supply chain: 100% of TSMC's ultrapure water (UPW) plants for wafers at 10nm and below are built and operated by Organo. Across all TSMC nodes, Organo has 70-80% share of TSMC's water treatment installations. Ultrapure water is not a commodity — at the cutting edge, semiconductor-grade UPW cannot contain impurities beyond 1 part per trillion (10^12). Any contamination in UPW systems directly destroys chip yields, making this a mission-critical, zero-tolerance application. TSMC recognized Organo with its 2024 Excellent Performance Award, underscoring the depth of this partnership. Organo operates approximately 100 semiconductor UPW plants across 8 countries, making it the world's #2 semiconductor UPW provider with 20-30% global market share (behind Kurita Water at 35-45%, ahead of Nomura Micro Science at 10-15%). The top 3 combined control 80%+ of the global market.

GROWTH FLYWHEEL — TSMC GLOBAL EXPANSION: The investment thesis hinges on a powerful flywheel: every new TSMC fab anywhere in the world generates near-automatic revenue for Organo. Current expansion projects include: (1) TSMC JASM Kumamoto, Japan — Phase 1 operational December 2024, Phase 2 by end 2027; (2) TSMC Arizona, USA — Organo USA Inc. (established 2021) opened an Arizona office specifically to serve this project and prospect other US semiconductor customers; (3) TSMC ESMC Dresden, Germany — Organo executing water treatment projects for TSMC's European expansion. Each advanced fab requires a bespoke UPW system costing hundreds of millions of yen, with a 1.5-2 year lead time and ongoing service contracts thereafter. FY2026/3 orders guidance of 185B JPY (+22.3% YoY) directly reflects this pipeline.

RAPIDUS — THE NEXT FRONTIER: Organo has secured the water purification contract for Rapidus, Japan's national champion for 2nm-class chip manufacturing at its Chitose, Hokkaido fab. Uniquely, Organo will not just build but also own and operate the UPW facility at the Rapidus site — a recurring-revenue model that locks in long-term cash flows beyond one-time project revenue. This build-own-operate approach represents a strategic evolution toward higher-margin, annuity-like revenue streams.

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE: Kurita Water Industries (6370.T) is the #1 global semiconductor UPW provider by revenue (35-45% share) and is the dominant competitor. Kurita is stronger in standardized equipment and aggressive pricing, with deeper penetration in North America and Europe. However, Organo's advantage is relationship depth with TSMC — Kurita has not been able to displace Organo at advanced TSMC nodes. Nomura Micro Science (6254.T) holds 10-15% global share and is strong in Korea (Samsung is its main customer). The Samsung relationship is with Nomura, not Organo — a critical nuance. The competitive landscape shifted in December 2025 when Ecolab (ECL) acquired Ovivo's Electronics UPW business for $1.8B, creating a new major US-based competitor with ~$500M in semiconductor UPW revenue. Xylem acquired Evoqua for $7.5B in 2023, adding another well-resourced Western competitor. Veolia (France) co-dominates the broader global UPW market.

CHINA EXPOSURE & GEOGRAPHIC MIX: Revenue breakdown is Japan 60.3%, Taiwan 17.1% (JPY 27.9B), China 12.6%, rest-of-world 10%. Greater China exposure totals ~30% when including Taiwan. Chinese clients likely include SMIC and Hua Hong Semiconductor (China's #1 and #2 foundries). While China revenue provides growth, it also introduces geopolitical risk — potential US-led export controls on semiconductor equipment could indirectly affect UPW system sales to Chinese fabs.

FINANCIAL PROFILE: FY2025/3 delivered strong results: revenue 163.3B JPY (+8.6% YoY), operating profit 31.1B JPY (+38.0% YoY). Operating margin of 19.1% significantly exceeds the G1 Equipment peer median of 14.9% (1.28x), reflecting the high-value nature of UPW systems. ROE stands at an impressive 21.7% — nearly 3x the G1 peer average of 7.6% — indicating exceptional capital efficiency. FY2026/3 guidance calls for revenue of 175B JPY (+7.2%), operating profit of 36B JPY (+15.7%), and net income of 27B JPY. The relatively moderate revenue growth guidance masks the underlying strength: project revenue recognition follows percentage-of-completion accounting with 1.5-2 year lead times, meaning today's order surge (185B JPY projected orders) will flow through revenue over the next 2-3 years.

SERVICE & SOLUTIONS — RECURRING REVENUE EVOLUTION: Beyond one-time project construction, Organo is strategically growing its service and solutions business (maintenance, chemical supply, operational support for existing UPW plants). With ~100 semiconductor UPW plants under its belt, each requiring ongoing water treatment chemicals, membrane replacement, and system optimization, this recurring revenue base provides earnings stability and margin expansion potential. The Rapidus build-own-operate model is the logical extension of this strategy.

OWNERSHIP STRUCTURE & FLOAT: Tosoh Corporation (4042.T), a major chemical company, has held a ~44% stake in Organo since 1955. This parent relationship provides strategic benefits (Tosoh produces ion exchange resins via Tosoh Bioscience) but limits free float to approximately 54%. Foreign ownership at 22.5% is well below the expected ~45% for a company with near-maximum moat scores (B=24/25), suggesting significant potential for institutional buying as global investors discover this TSMC-linked play.

VALUATION CONCERN: The market has aggressively re-rated Organo. The stock has rallied +134% over the past year (from ~7,100 to 16,620 JPY, touching an all-time high of 16,950). PBR at 5.85x is an extreme premium — 2.35x the G1 Equipment peer median of 2.49x. This makes Organo the most expensive stock by PBR in the G1 peer group. PER at 27.1x still offers a modest 14% discount to the G1 median of 31.59x, but this is cold comfort given the PBR stretched valuation. Dividend yield at 1.14% is minimal. The stock trades at 98% of its 52-week high, and both analysts covering the stock have Buy ratings but with an average target of 16,250 JPY — below the current price. The valuation case has substantially deteriorated.

CATALYSTS: (1) Foreign ownership gap — at 22.5% vs expected 45%+, there is ~22.5pp of potential institutional buying as global semiconductor fund managers recognize Organo as a pure-play TSMC proxy. This is the strongest near-term catalyst. (2) TSMC fab ramp-up — revenue from Kumamoto Phase 2, Arizona, and Dresden will flow through over 2026-2028. (3) Rapidus build-own-operate model — if successful, could be replicated at future fab projects, transforming the revenue profile toward higher-margin recurring streams. (4) Japan semiconductor policy support — government subsidies for domestic fab construction directly benefit Organo's pipeline. (5) AI-driven semiconductor capex boom — every AI accelerator chip (NVIDIA, AMD, custom ASICs) requires advanced fab capacity, which requires Organo's UPW systems.

RISKS: (1) TSMC concentration — while TSMC is only 14% of total revenue, it represents 70-80% of semiconductor UPW business. A deterioration in the TSMC relationship would be catastrophic. (2) Tosoh parent control — with 44% ownership, Tosoh controls corporate governance; minority shareholders have limited influence. Tosoh has no current plans to divest, but the concentrated ownership suppresses float and could limit shareholder-friendly capital allocation. (3) Geopolitical exposure — 29.7% of revenue from Greater China (Taiwan + mainland) is at risk from escalating US-China tensions, potential Taiwan Strait disruption, or expanded semiconductor equipment export controls. (4) Kurita competitive threat — Kurita is larger, better-capitalized, and more globally diversified; if TSMC sought to reduce supplier concentration, Kurita would be the primary beneficiary. (5) Stretched valuation — at 5.85x PBR and near all-time highs, any earnings miss or guidance disappointment could trigger sharp corrections. (6) Forex sensitivity — international expansion increases USD/JPY and TWD/JPY exposure.

CONCLUSION: Organo is the definitive 'TSMC infrastructure play' — owning 100% of TSMC's advanced-node UPW installations creates a competitive position that is effectively unassailable at existing fabs and self-reinforcing at new ones. The B=24/25 moat score, 19.1% operating margin, and 21.7% ROE reflect a genuinely superior business. However, the market has fully discovered and re-priced this story: at 5.85x PBR and +134% YoY appreciation, the stock has run well ahead of near-term fundamentals. The foreign ownership gap (22.5% vs expected 45%) remains the strongest catalyst, suggesting further institutional accumulation. This is a 'right company, expensive price' situation. Suitable for a watchlist position with entry on meaningful corrections (target entry below 13,000 JPY / 4x PBR), or as a small position for investors willing to pay up for what is arguably the most defensible TSMC supply chain position outside of ASML. Long-term investors should focus on the 185B JPY order pipeline, which ensures revenue visibility through FY2028. [Score: 60.0/100 | Confidence: High — 100% data coverage]

companies.name: Daifuku Co., Ltd.

B.note: B4 updated from graph.json; others from prior scoring

F.note: Scored from isForexSensitive, client arrays, descriptions

companies.investmentThesis: Daifuku is the world's dominant provider of automated material handling systems (AMHS) and overhead transport (OHT) for semiconductor fabs — the internal logistics backbone that moves wafers between process steps. Every new fab built globally (TSMC Arizona, Samsung Texas, Intel Ohio, Rapidus Hokkaido) requires Daifuku's systems, creating a direct and durable leverage to the global fab construction boom driven by AI chip demand. Switching costs are maximal once a fab's AMHS is installed. The company scores perfectly on market share and switching costs. As the global fab capex cycle accelerates through 2026-2028, Daifuku's order backlog is structurally positioned to grow. Financial data needed to validate entry timing. [Confidence: Very Low — no financial data available]
